coredata.dc:title: 23 Phthalocyanine-nanocarbon materials and their composites: Preparation, properties, and applications
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2019 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Şenocak, Ahmet
Demirbaş, Erhan
Durmuş, Mahmut
coredata.dc:description: This chapter addresses recent advances in the development of phthalocyanine (Pcs)-modified fullerene, carbon nanotubes, and graphene hybrid nanomaterials. These nanomaterials are prepared by covalent chemical bonding or noncovalent interactions between carbon-based nanostructures and phthalocyanine derivatives. These hybrid nanomaterials are useful for different technological applications such as solar cells, chemical/bio/gas sensors, fuel cells, and electronic devices. At this point, molecular materials, including combinations of carbon nanostructures with phthalocyanine derivatives, are currently being evaluated because of their novel materials and composites with their unique optoelectronic properties. For this reason, this chapter will be useful for readers in different areas from the academic and industrial communities.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hybrid materials
Nanocarbon structures
Nanocomposite
Phthalocyanine
Fullerene
Carbon nanotubes
Graphene

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Functionalization of nanomaterials with phthalocyanine derivatives

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structures of carbon materials.
The major isomers of fullerenes (C60, C70, and C80).
Structures of single walled (SWCNTs), double walled (DWCNTs) and multi walled (MWCNTs) carbon nanotubes.
Structures of single and multilayer graphene.
Structures of phthalocyanines.
Fullerene conjugated unsymmetrically substituted NiPc compounds.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 23.7

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Normalized absorption spectra of P3HT:PCBM, pristine SiPC-0, and SiNC-1 in films; (B) energy level diagram of the components of ternary and quaternary blend solar cells; (C) chemical structures of SiPC-0 and SiPC-1 (with 0 or 1 CH2 linkers to the axial pyrene groups), and SiNC-1.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced from Ke L, Gasparini N, Min J, Zhang H, Adam M, Rechberger S, et al. Panchromaticternary/quaternary polymer/fullerene BHJ solar cells based on novel siliconnaphthalocyanine and silicon Phthalocyanine dye sensitizers. J Mater Chem A 2017;5:2550–62, with kind permission of RSC.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fullerene porphyrin/phthalocyanine and (1) fullerene phthalocyanine (2) triads [31].
Nonhomeotropic Pc-C60 dyads [32].

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 23.10

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Structures of phthalocyanines and tetrabenzotriazacorroles, and (B) energy levels of the materials relative to the vacuum level.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced from Raboui H, Thibau ES, Josey DS, Lu ZH, Bender TP. Oxy phosphorustetrabenzotriazacorrole: firming up the chemical structure and identifying organicphotovoltaic functionality to leverage its uniquedual absorbance. J Mater Chem 2017;A5:10978–10985, with kind permission of RSC.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structures of PTB7, ZnPc, and PC71BM in the ternary bulk heterojunction.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 23.12

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Functionalization of the sidewalls of SWNTs with Pc molecule (A) and its TEM image (B).

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced from Ballesteros B, de la Torre G, Ehli C, Rahman GMA, Agullo-Rueda F, Guldi DM, et al. Single-wall carbon nanotubes bearing covalently linkedphthalocyanines-photoinduced electron transfer. J Am Chem Soc 2007;129:5061–68, with kind permission of ACS.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 23.13

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Covalently bonded Cu(II)Pc-MWCNT hybrid and its TEM image.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced from Yang Z, Pu H, Yuan J, Wan D, Liu Y. Phthalocyanines-MWCNT hybrid materials:fabrication, aggregation and photoconductivity properties improvement. Chem Phys Lett 2008;465:73–77, with kind permission of Elsevier.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic route toward Pc-SWCNT hybrid materials by Click reaction [48].
(A) Asymmetrical and symmetrical zinc phthalocyanine compounds. (B) SWCNTs- zinc(II) phthalocyanine hybrid material.
Synthesized CuPcR4 derivatives.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of the covalently bonded ZnPc:SWCNT hybrid material.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced from Kadem B, Göksel M, Şenocak A, Demirbaş E, Atilla D, Durmuş M, et al. Effect of covalent and noncovalent linking on the structure, optical and electrical propertiesof zinc(II) phthalocyanine functionalized carbon nanomaterials. Polyhedron 2016;110:37–45, with kind permission of Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 23.18

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of thin films of ZnPc:SWCNT noncovalent (A), ZnPc:SWCNT covalent (B), ZnPc:rGO noncovalent (C), and ZnPc:rGO covalent (D) hybrids.

ce:figure.ce:source: From Polyakov MS, Basova T, Göksel M, Şenocak A, Demirbaş E, Durmuş M, et al. Effect of covalent and noncovalent linking on the sensor properties of zinc(II)phthalocyanine functionalised carbon nanomaterials. Synth Met 2017;227:78–86, with kind permission of Elsevier.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic route to Zn(II)Pc [55].
The structures of the hybrids of the M2HnPc2, M2Pc2, and M2Pc2-CNTs.
A schematic illustration of the columnar tetrasulfonate-substituted Cu(II)Pcs on MWCNTs.
The synthesized MTnPc, MPc, and CNTs-CONH-MPc.
The synthesized polymeric CNTs-Pc hybrid materials.
Graphene-Pc nanoconjugate.
Conjugation of cobalt mono carboxy phenoxy phthalocyanine to graphene oxide nanosheets.
(A) Procedure for the covalent functionalization of graphene with phthalocyanine [78], and (B) the structure of the phthalocyanine-pyrene conjugate [79].

Structural illustrations of GQD-FePc hybrids.
Pyrene-derivatized Zn(II) and Co(II) phthalocyanines and porphyrins.
Phthalocyanine-fullerene hybrids and their applications
Phthalocyanine-CNTs hybrids and their applications

row.entry: Spin-filter transport and magnetoresistance effects ratio of nearly 100%
Fuel cells and batteries (power density of 185mWcm−2)
Electron transfer (the higher HOMO and LUMO gaps and electronic transfer efficiencies)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Isoniazid is one of the most efficient and widely used drugs for treatment of tuberculosis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Phthalocyanine-graphene hybrids and their applications

row.entry: Biothiols fluorescence sensors Cys 0.30, Hcys 0.45 and GSH 0.86nmolL−1

chapter.ce:title: Phthalocyanine-nanocarbon materials and their composites: Preparation, properties, and applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Gebze Technical University, Department of Chemistry, Gebze, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This chapter addresses recent advances in the development of phthalocyanine (Pcs)-modified fullerene, carbon nanotubes, and graphene hybrid nanomaterials. These nanomaterials are prepared by covalent chemical bonding or noncovalent interactions between carbon-based nanostructures and phthalocyanine derivatives. These hybrid nanomaterials are useful for different technological applications such as solar cells, chemical/bio/gas sensors, fuel cells, and electronic devices. At this point, molecular materials, including combinations of carbon nanostructures with phthalocyanine derivatives, are currently being evaluated because of their novel materials and composites with their unique optoelectronic properties. For this reason, this chapter will be useful for readers in different areas from the academic and industrial communities.

ce:para.ce:bold: Functionalization of nanomaterials with phthalocyanine derivatives

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials comprise a diverse range of carbon allotropes with a number of sizes and shapes. This impressive development of new carbon nanoforms has its origin in the discovery of fullerenes by H. Kroto, R. Smalley, and R. Curl; they received the Nobel Prize in 1996 for this discovery [1]. Therefore, it leads to the discovery of other important carbon nanostructures. In the last decade, carbon nanostructures have been utilized in major research areas such as solar cells, catalysts, sensors, and fuel cells because of their unique physical, chemical, optical, and electrical properties. Moreover, they are being used successfully in biomedical and sensing studies [2–4].

Carbon nanostructures show great diversity in structure such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, carbon dots, fullerene, carbon nanohorns, and graphene quantum dots (Fig. 23.1 ). These materials are layered materials and expressed as 0D (fullerene), 1D (one-dimensional nanotube), and 2D (two-dimensional graphene) with respect to the nanoscale range, <100nm (Fig. 23.1) [5].

Carbon is a versatile element and can form various allotropes, including graphite, diamond, and fullerene-like structures. The entire cage shape of all carbon molecules is called a fullerene, which has attracted considerable attention due to the discovery of buckyballs in 1985. Its molecules are spherical and contain 60 carbon atoms. C60 is the lightest of the three crystalline carbon forms with a density of 1.7g/cm3 compared to 2.3 for graphite and 3.5 for diamond. As the only form of carbon that is soluble in common organic solvents, the smaller fullerenes, including C60, can be dispersed in a solution for the purposes of impregnation and spray coating. Another important property of the C60 molecule is its high symmetry. There are 120 symmetrical operations such as rotation around an axis and reflection in a plane that make C60 the most symmetrical molecule. Fullerenes are extremely strong molecules because fullerenes can withstand collisions of up to 15,000mph against stainless steel, merely bouncing back and keeping their shapes [6]. Applications of fullerene with its functionalized derivatives are increasing in different potential areas such as pharmaceuticals, medicine, photovoltaics, etc. Other fullerenes were discovered shortly afterward with more and fewer carbon atoms ranging from 18 atoms to hundreds of atoms. C60 remains the easiest to produce and the cheapest with prices rising rapidly for other larger fullerenes shown in Fig. 23.2 .

CNTs categorized as single, double, and multiwalled nanotubes (Fig. 23.3 ) are members of the fullerene family (spherical in shape) whereas CNTs are potentially useful in a wide variety of applications such as solar cells, sensors, nanoelectronics, optics, etc., due to their cylindrical shape with unique properties. CNTs exhibit high surface areas, large aspect ratios, and unique electrical, mechanical, and thermal properties [7, 8]. These important specifications make CNTs useful as fillers in different polymers, ceramics, and metallic surfaces. Moreover, applications of CNTs have great potential such as nanomedicine, actuators, capacitors, sensors, etc. [9, 10]. The chemical bonding of CNTs involves sp2 hybrid carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice. Hence, the free electrons in each unit are delocalized over all atoms. Carbon nanotubes are the strongest and stiffest materials based on tensile strength and elastic modulus. These properties are related to the covalent sp2 bonds formed between the individual carbon atoms. [10–13].

The thermal conductivity of CNTs depends on morphology, atomic arrangement, impurities, and the diameter and length of the tubes. The thermal conductivity values of CNTs are generally based on theoretical simulations and calculations from indirect experiments due to technological difficulties related to nanoscale experimental measurements. The conductivities of SWCNTs and MWCNTs are around 2000 and 3000W/mK, respectively. [14, 15].

Graphene belongs to a big family of graphite-like materials. Graphene is a single layer of carbon atoms tightly packed into a 2D honeycomb crystal lattice. Graphene is more robust than many conventional materials, including steel. The term graphene should be used only when the reactions and structural relations or other properties of individual layers are discussed [16]. Graphene was characterized as a 2D crystalline material in many published studies before 2004. Ultrathin graphitic films were also reported to be produced with monolayer graphene [17, 18].

A very simple methodology to produce and observe microscopic few-layer graphene on oxidized silicon wafers was suggested by Novoselov et al. [19], who used a Scotch tape approach (Fig. 23.4 ). This approach was recognized as a protocol for 2D transport studies of single-layer graphene samples [19]. The isolation of “pristine” graphene without heteroatomic contamination was also reported by Novoselov et al. [19]. The rest of the properties for graphene are optical transparency of 97.7%, electron mobility of 200,000cm2 V−1 s−1, thermal conductivity of 5000W/mK−1, a surface area of 2630m2 g−1, and a breaking strength of 42Nm−1 [20–22]. Therefore, graphene has captured the imagination of scientists and is now a highly active area in a plethora of fields.

Graphene is being used in a number of applications, including anticorrosion coatings and paints, efficient and precise sensors, microelectronics batteries and supercapacitor electrode materials, faster DNA sequencing, fluorescence bioimaging, photosensitizers in photodynamic therapy (PDT), and drug delivery, owing to its interesting properties [23, 24].

Phthalocyanines (Pcs) are organic molecules that consist of four isoindole subunits linked together through nitrogen atoms. Metal-free phthalocyanine is a planar molecule that contains two hydrogen atoms in its central cavity. Phthalocyanine, classified as an aromatic macrocyclic compound, has a formula of H2Pc (Fig. 23.5 ). Pcs are 2D macrocycles molecules that can be self-assembled into stacks through π-π supramolecular interactions [25, 26]. Pcs and its derivatives show characteristic color as well as photo- and electrochemical properties as well as two characteristic absorption bands, a Soret band (300–400nm) and a Q-band (600–800nm). The outstanding chemical and physical properties of phthalocyanines are employed for uses such as pigments and dyes, photosensitizers in photodynamic therapy, quantum computing, chemical and biosensors, molecular photovoltaics, and liquid crystals. They are models for biologically important species such as porphyrins, hemoglobin, and chlorophyll [27].

Intense absorption of Pcs in the red/near-infrared (NIR) region of the solar spectrum due to their redox chemistry and high fluorescence quantum yields renders them ideal candidates for light-harvesting antenna systems. These unique physical properties, coupled with the synthetic versatility of these macrocycles, have prompted the utilization of these compounds as multifunctional materials in donor acceptor ensembles.

Unsubstituted Pcs and many of its complexes have very low solubility in organic solvents. Benzene dissolves less than a mg of H2Pc per liter at 40°C. H2Pc is easily dissolved in sulfuric acid due to the protonation of the nitrogen atoms bridging the pyrrole rings. Many Pc compounds are thermally stable and do not melt but may be sublimed. Moreover, substituted Pc complexes have much higher solubility. Unsubstituted Pcs materials are green or blue in the visible region because they absorb light strongly between 600 and 700nm. The substitution can shift the absorption toward the longer wavelengths, causing the color to change from blue to green to colorless [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of nanomaterials with phthalocyanine derivatives

ce:section.ce:para: Fullerene is soluble on its own solvents such as toluene. Thus, a solution of unfunctionalized fullerene could be incorporated in the gel matrices to form gel-nanocomposites through π-π stacking interaction between fullerene and gelator molecules. However, sometimes phase separation of fullerene occurs from the composite and thus covalent functionalization is undertaken in order to increase the interaction with the host matrix. Covalent functionalization of fullerene is mostly achieved through cycloadditions or nucleophilic addition reactions, and the resulting gel nanomaterials are shown to have intimate interactions between the gelator and fullerene. Functionalization helps to increase its solubility, reduce the phase separation of fullerene molecules from the mixture, and optimize the band gap.

The first Pc-C60 conjugate was introduced in 1997. The Diels-Alder reaction was used to covalently combine Ni(II)Pc with a spherical C60 carbon nanostructure from the butadiene substituent of the macrocycles, as shown in Fig. 23.6
[29]. Since then, many other supramolecular and covalent complexes of Pc-C60 have been extensively investigated. The parameters such as electronic properties of the Pc, the chemical features of the link group between C60 and Pc, and supramolecular interaction patterns were necessary to assemble two groups in Pc-C60 noncovalent systems.

Ke et al. developed a silicon phthalocyanines (SiPc-0 and SiPc-1) and naphthalocyanine (SiNC-1) (Fig. 23.7C) with axial substituents consisting of pyrene carboxylic acid groups for the development of high-efficiency organic solar cells based on polymer-fullerene blends (Fig. 23.7B). A ternary organic photovoltaic (OPV) device as a photosensitizer in the longer wavelength region has been constructed with SiNC-1. SiNC-1 gives an additional charge generation pathway and presents strong photosensitivity at 650–950nm (Fig. 23.7A). It produced an increase up to 10% for short-circuit current density and 24% for power conversion efficiency (PCE) in the blend with P3HT/PCBM [30].

Hoa et al. described the synthesis and characterization of two new fullerene porphyrin/phthalocyanine (1) and fullerene-phthalocyanine (2) to obtain electrochemical devices (Fig. 23.8 ). The dendrons bearing the chromophores and the fullerene derivatives were synthesized via Click reaction. The properties of the C60/ZnP-ZnPc (1) and C60/ZnPc2 (2) were studied by absorption spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry. Only weak interactions were detected for both compounds by absorption spectroscopy. The electrochemical properties suggested that the C60 moiety better interacts with the ZnP-ZnPc dendron than with the pure ZnPc dendron; this was also collaborated by the reduction of intensity of the Soret band of the porphyrin [31].

Ishikawa et al. synthesized novel liquid crystalline Pc-C60 dyads (C n S)6PcCu-C60 (n =14, 16, 18) in order to investigate the mesomorphism and alignment behavior of them (Fig. 23.9 ). These compounds showed liquid crystalline donor-acceptor dyads, connected alkylthio-substituted CuPc, and fullerene. This supramolecular structure is compatible with the 1D nanoarray structure so that the present Pc-C60 dyads are very favorable for application to organic thin film solar cells to raise the conversion efficiency [32].

Raboui et al. investigated the potential of tetrabenzotriazacorroles (Tbc) in OPV applications (Fig. 23.10A ). The functionality of the Tbc compound as an organic electronic material in OPV devices was determined through the pairing with fullerenes, C60 and C70 (Fig. 23.10B). The properties of oxy phosphorus tetrabenzotriazacorrole were also explored in OPV devices. A 10nm thick POTbc layer resulted in a PCE of 1.96% [33].

M. Stylianakis et al. fabricated ternary organic photovoltaic devices. The ZnPc acts as an electron cascade material, providing an efficient energy level offset between the polymeric donor and the fullerene derivative acceptor, enhancing charge transfer, reducing exciton recombination, and thus improving the photovoltaic performance of the devices (Fig. 23.11 ). The fabricated ternary organic solar cells led to a PCE of 8.52%, increased by ~15% compared to the reference binary cell [34].

Recently, the preparation and application of Pcs-fullerene based on hybrid materials have increased significantly. Table 23.1 presents both preparation methods for Pcs-fullerene hybrids and their applications.

Pristine CNTs and graphenes have limited solubility in common solvents. Thus, their applications are often restricted because of their poor solubility in spite of having interesting properties. Therefore, the functionalization of nanocarbons is mainly carried out to increase their dispersibility in a particular solvent. There are mainly two strategies for functionalization: (i) covalent and (ii) noncovalent. General strategies for the covalent functionalization of CNTs and graphene include oxidation with acids, amidation/esterification of COOH groups obtained by oxidation, Huisgen cycloadditions (Click), the addition of free radicals to sp2 carbon atoms, etc. Thus, the resulting functionalized materials are easily dispersible in common solvents. Incorporation of these substances into the gel matrix can form hybrid gel-nanocomposites due to the additional interaction through the added functionality with the gelator molecules. The added functionality promotes even nonaromatic gelators for the gel-nanocomposite synthesis. Therefore, covalent functionalization, even though it introduces defects and thus affects the performance of the materials in certain areas such as electronic applications, eventually helps to form intimate hybrid materials by a range of different gelators, leading to improved mechanical properties.

There are a number of studies in the literature about functionalization of CNT with Pc derivatives (Fig. 23.12A). Oxidized SWCNTs were functionalized with hydroxymethyl-substituted Pc by an esterification reaction, and this procedure has pioneered other functionalization studies. A TEM image of ZnPc-SWCNT (Fig. 23.12B) reveals less-bundled materials than what is typically found for pristine SWCNTs. This observation is consistent with SWCNT functionalization with phthalocyanines, where Pc rings may disrupt the strong interactions between individual SWCNTs [46].

Another approach lies in the preparation of Pc-MWCNTs through a reaction of carboxylic acid, including esterification of the acid groups with 1,6-hexanediol. Ipso substitution of 4-nitrophthalonitrile with a free hydroxyl group is bonded to MWCNTs (Fig. 23.13 ). A Cu(II)Pc-MWCNT hybrid is obtained with phthalonitrile-substituted MWCNTs reacted with a substituted phthalonitrile in the presence of a copper salt that is soluble in a variety of organic solvents. The Cu(II)Pc-MWCNT hybrid material showed better photoconductivity than pristine Cu(II)Pc and a Cu(II)Pc/MWCNT blended composite [47].

Campidelli et al. focused on developing effective strategies to produce Pc-SWCNT hybrid materials using Huisgen cycloaddition to efficiently functionalize nanotubes with Zn(II)Pcs [48]. First, ethynyl-containing SWCNTs were prepared by Tour reaction. Subsequently, the reaction between the azido-functionalized Zn(II)Pc and the ethynyl-terminated CNTs was catalyzed with Cu(I) to yield Pc-SWCNT (Fig. 23.14 ).

Our research group has reported many studies on carbon and phthalocyanine hybrid nanomaterials and investigated their properties in different areas such as sensors, solar cells, and liquid crystals. The dispersion of SWCNTs in liquid crystalline asymmetric Zn(II) phthalocyanine bearing one pyrene and six polyoxy units (AB3 type) and symmetric Zn(II) phthalocyanine bearing eight polyoxy units (B4 type) was carried out (Fig. 23.15 ). Both pure compounds and their composites with SWCNTs showed liquid crystalline properties with rectangular columnar stacking at room temperature [49].

The same hybrid materials were applied for sensor application in 2014. The pyrene group was selected to enhance the interaction of Pc molecules with SWCNTs. Thin films of SWCNTs and SWCNT/ZnPc were prepared by drop casting onto interdigitated electrodes. Then they were used as active layers to detect ammonia vapor (1–200ppm) by measuring the changes in electrical resistance. The comparative sensor response of SWCNTs and films of the SWCNT/ZnPc toward ammonia vapor was performed to identify the synergic effect between ZnPc derivatives and SWCNTs. It was confirmed that the response of the hybrid films with pyrene substitutes to the ammonia vapor is twice that obtained by hybrid films with ZnPc. [50]. Phthalocyanine was modified with CNTs for obtaining the optical detection of amine vapors (Fig. 23.16 ). Thin films of the peripheral and nonperipheral tetra-substituted copper phthalocyanines [CuPcR4] and their hybrids with SWCNT were evaluated for the detection of optical active layers of amine vapors (methylamine, dimethylamine, and trimethylamine). The adsorption of amines onto the surfaces of the film was determined by changes in the phase shift from the spectra of the total internal reflection ellipsometry. Among the rest of the amines, methylamine presented a higher sensitivity and lower response time. The interaction with film surfaces decreased with the steric hindrance from shapes of the secondary and tertiary amine molecules. In this study, the sensitivity of SWCNT/CuPcR4 for all active layers was reported as higher than that of CuPcR4 films. The lower response time was 66s at 40ppm and the highest sensitivity toward methylamine vapor was recorded as 0.000325/ppm [51].

In another study in 2016, covalently and noncovalently functionalized SWCNTs with an asymmetrically substituted ZnPc were synthesized for comparison purposes (Fig. 23.17 ). The noncovalently and covalently functionalized ZnPc-SWCNTs were obtained by mixing SWCNTs with ZnPc in an ultrasonic bath and the reaction of azido-substituted SWCNTs by terminally alkyne-bearing ZnPc via Click reaction. The electrical conductivity noticeably increased to a value of 218.6mS/cm because its value is 11.4mS/cm in the pure ZnPc film when ZnPc was functionalized covalently with SWCNTs [52].

Buber and coworkers fabricated an amperometric biosensor for the detection of glucose oxidase with conducting polymer-MWCNTs and water soluble ZnPc (Fig. 23.19 ). The constructed biosensor showed a linear response for glucose between 0.025 and 1.0mM with a detection limit of 0.018mM [55].

Gao et al. modified carbon nanotubes (CNTs) chemically by a series of binuclear hexamino-metallo phthalocyanines (M2Pc2), which were produced by reducing the corresponding binuclear hexanitro-metallophthalocyanines (M2H n Pc2) (M=Mn(II), Fe(II), Co(II), Ni(II), Cu(II), Zn(II)) (Fig. 23.20 ). The electrocatalytic performance of CNTs bonding with M2Pc2 (M2Pc2-CNTs) to a Li/SOCl2 battery was tested by adding these compounds into the electrolyte. The capacities of the battery were increased in the range of 59.1%–128.5%. In addition, the sequence of the electrochemical catalytic performance of M2Pc2-CNTs is as follows: Fe>Co>Cu>Zn>Ni>Mn. The CNTs bonding with M2Pc2 can greatly promote electronic transmission, which significantly improves the performance and the initial voltage of the Li/SOCl2 battery. CV measurements are applied to verify the M2Pc2-CNTs possessing catalytic performances and a reasonable mechanism is proposed [56].

Interactions through hydrogen bonding between tetrasulfonate-substituted Cu(II)Pc and oxidized MWCNTs were obtained as electron acceptor components by Hatton et al. Spectroscopic and morphological studies showed that the Pc cores stacked in columns with the MWCNT scaffold (Fig. 23.21 ) [57].

The CNTs were chemically modified by MPcs for the catalytic activity of CNTs-CONH-MPc, (Fig. 23.22 ). The MPcs bonded chemically with CNTs and the obtained hybrid materials showed good catalytic activity. The results indicated that all catalysts improved the cell capacity of Li/SOCl2 up to 95.84mAh (88.49%) [58].

The surface of MWCNTs may be functionalized by a cyclization reaction between nitrile-modified MWCNTs and bis-phthalonitrile in order to obtain high-performance CNT-based polymer nanocomposites (Fig. 23.23 ). To achieve this, Pcs were coated on the surface of MWCNTs, giving rise to strong interfacial adhesion and good dispersion between MWNCTs and the poly(arylene ether nitrile) (PEN) matrix. The tensile modulus and tensile strength of PEN nanocomposites with Pc-MWCNTs (1:50) increased from 85.6 to 108MPa and from 2300 to 3350MPa, respectively. A low rheological percolation threshold of 0.69wt% was achieved and the value of the dielectric constant of PEN nanocomposites was 3.3 for PEN and 16.6 with Pc-MWCNTs (1:20wt%) [58].
Table 23.2 illustrated the preparation methods for Pc-CNTs and their applications.

Graphene is an outstanding material because it consists of a 2D single layer of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms bonded in a hexagonal honeycomb lattice. Graphene has the same structure of carbon atoms to form carbon nanotubes, but graphene is flat rather than cylindrical. It has exceptional properties, including mechanical and thermal properties. Graphene is also an extremely diverse material, and can be combined with other elements to obtain different graphene-based materials with various superior properties. Graphene and graphene oxide (GO) have many applications such as catalysis, chemical and biological sensing, energy storage, and electronics. A series of coordination and organometallic compounds has been successfully prepared with graphene by covalent or noncovalent modes [23, 54]. Regarding the covalent functionalization of graphene (Fig. 23.24 ), the absence of strained carbon atoms makes the material less reactive than fullerenes [76]. Electronic interactions between the graphene layers and Pc molecules were determined by the steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopic techniques.

Graphene was functionalized with phthalocyanine by a covalent amide and esterification reaction in the literature. The noncovalent functionalization was based on π-π interactions. Cobalt mono carboxyphenoxy phthalocyanine (CoMCPhPc), either covalently linked to graphene oxide nanosheets (GONS), sulfur-doped graphene oxide nanosheets (SDGONS), nitrogen-doped graphene oxide nanosheets (NDGONS), or sulfur/nitrogen codoped graphene oxide nanosheets (SNDGONS) or sequentially added, was used to modify a glassy carbon electrode for detection of hydrogen peroxide (Fig. 23.25 ). Noncovalent CoMCPhPc-SNDGONS-GCE and covalent CoMCPhPc-GCE resulted in good limits of detection and catalytic rate constant values were found 1.58 and 5.44nM, respectively [77].

Ragoussi et al. managed to obtain highly exfoliated graphene covalently linked to electron-accepting phthalocyanines (Fig. 23.26A). The functionalization of the nanocarbon surface with alkylsulfonyl phthalocyanines was attained by means of a Click chemistry. The new ensemble was fully characterized by thermogravimetric analysis, AFM, TEM, and Raman as well as ground-state absorption. A series of steady-state and time-resolved spectroscopy experiments resulted in photo-induced electron transfer from the graphene to the electron-accepting phthalocyanines. This was the first example of an electron donor-acceptor nanoconjugate character of graphene [78]. Another photo-induced charge separation was described by Roth et al. The photophysical properties of the resulting nanohybrid were obtained with synthesis of a zinc alkylsulfonylphthalocyanine pyrene conjugate (Fig. 23.26B). The pyrene unit in the conjugate was important for the determination of noncovalently immobilizing phthalocyanines onto the exfoliated graphite. Strong interactions at ground and excited states dominated the electronic properties of the synthesized nanohybrid. As an example, femtosecond pump probe experiments helped generate an ultrafast charge separation [79]. A covalently linked Pc-graphene group was prepared by a Click reaction between phenylethynyl derivatized graphene and an azido-ZnPc.

Click modification on a graphene surface with phthalocyanine was investigated for the effect of covalent and noncovalent linking on the carbon nanostructure (Fig. 23.27 ). The solar cell and ammonia sensor properties of this graphene-ZnPc hybrids were determined [52–54]. The pure polyoxy modified asymmetric zinc(II) phthalocyanine (ZnPc) film without graphene present provided an electrical conductivity of 11.4mS/cm. This value increased noticeably when reduced graphene oxide was covalently (53.1mS/cm) and noncovalently (51.7mS/cm) bonded with ZnPc. In addition, the effects on organic solar cell performance are obtained for rGO covalently and noncovalently functionalized by ZnPc. Their solar cell photo current efficiencies with covalently and noncovalently rGO are found to increase from 3.46% to 4.7%.

Koh and coworkers synthesized iron(II) phthalocyanine (FePc)-GQD conjugates for the energy conversion systems of fuel cells (Fig. 23.28 ). This material exhibited a greatly enhanced onset potential via a four-electron pathway in an alkaline electrolyte. Moreover, the synthesized electrocatalyst showed distinguished tolerance toward methanol and carbon monoxide [80].

Pyrene-derivatized Zn or Co phthalocyanines (Pcs) and porphyrins (Ps) were immobilized on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to form GQDs-Pcs and GQDs-Ps hybrids via the π-π stacking interaction method (Fig. 23.29 ) [81]. The resultant hybrids were stable between GQDs and Pcs/Ps. These materials may be important for studies in both fundamental and applied perspectives, owing to the synergistic contributions from the combination of their electronic and optical properties.
Preparations of phthalocyanine-graphene hybrids and their applications are shown in Table 23.3 .

Recent advances in the preparation of covalent and noncovalent fullerene-Pc, CNT-Pc, and graphene-Pc nanoconjugates and their increasing applications in many areas such as sensors, PDT, fuel cells, solar cells, etc., are highlighted. Pc-nanocarbon conjugates involving the phthalocyanine and nanocarbon moieties have a great success on the chemical protocols toward the preparation of these systems using different reactions such as “Click”, esterification, amidation. Moreover, Pc-nanocarbon hybrid materials offer a myriad of incentives for their incorporation into many related areas due to their extraordinary conductivity, delocalized π electron systems, and stability features. Particularly, photo-induced unidirectional electron transfer processes occur in such Pc-nanocarbon systems, which is widely demonstrated by photophysical investigations. In this connection, hybrid materials based on the assembling of Pcs with nanocarbon will increase as they lead to novel materials and composites with special optoelectronic features.
